title_en: New York Is a Pickle Kind of Town

paragraphs.en: The Dutch authors of a new book, “The Pickled City: The Story of New York Pickles,” stop by the brined-delicacy staple Russ & Daughters.
paragraphs.zh: 这本新书《The Pickled City: The Story of New York Pickles》（《纽约腌菜之城：纽约腌菜的故事》）的荷兰作者们，来到老字号俄式食品店 Russ & Daughters 参观。

paragraphs.en: Not all foods endure—ortolan, aspic, candy cigarettes—but the pickle may be as close to immortal as a snack can get. The world’s oldest known cookbook, from around 1700 B.C.E. Mesopotamia, includes a recipe for a pickled-fish-and-grasshopper sauce. Shakespeare wrote, in “The Tempest,” about being “in a pickle”; pickles were served aboard the Titanic. Carlos Alcaraz and Frances Tiafoe have both been known to drink pickle juice in lieu of Gatorade—no unfair advantage there. The U.S. pickle industry has an estimated revenue of three billion dollars per year.
paragraphs.zh: 并不是所有食物都能经久不衰——有些像奥陶兰鸟（ortolan）、冻胶（aspic）、糖果香烟如今都已式微——但腌菜也许是零食里最接近不朽的一种了。世界上已知最古老的食谱来自公元前1700年前后的两河流域（美索不达米亚），其中就有一种腌鱼与蚱蜢酱的做法。莎士比亚在《暴风雨》中写到“in a pickle”；即便是泰坦尼克号上也曾上过腌菜。Carlos Alcaraz 和 Frances Tiafoe 都曾以喝腌菜汁替代佳得乐（Gatorade）而闻名——这并未带来不公平优势。美国腌菜行业每年据估计可带来三十亿美元的收入。

paragraphs.en: Paul van Ravestein and Monique Mulder, two principals at Mattmo Creative, a branding agency in Amsterdam, have become surprising experts in the preserved-cucumber field. In 2022, they published “De Zure Stad” (“The Sour City”), a history of Dutch pickles, then shifted focus across the Atlantic for “The Pickled City: The Story of New York Pickles.”
paragraphs.zh: Paul van Ravestein 和 Monique Mulder 是阿姆斯特丹一家品牌机构 Mattmo Creative 的两位合伙人，他们如今竟成为了醃黄瓜领域的“资深专家”。在2022年，他们出版了《De Zure Stad》（《酸之城》），这是一部荷兰腌菜历史著作；之后便将研究重心跨大西洋转向《The Pickled City: The Story of New York Pickles》（《纽约腌菜之城：纽约腌菜的故事》）。

paragraphs.en: “It’s an obsession, basically,” van Ravestein said the other day, in the hundred-and-twelve-year-old Russ & Daughters appetizing shop, on East Houston. During their New York pickle research, Mulder and van Ravestein had come upon a trove of tax-archive photographs from the early twentieth century, featuring a man posing with numbered placards in front of thousands of Lower East Side storefronts, nearly a hundred of which were pickle purveyors.
paragraphs.zh: 在东休斯顿街那家已有112年历史的 Russ & Daughters 经典小店里，van Ravestein 前些日子说：“这基本上是一种痴迷。” 在纽约腌菜的研究中，Mulder 和 van Ravestein 找到了二十世纪初的税务档案照片宝库：照片里一名男子在下东区成千上万家店铺前摆出姿势，手持编号木牌，其中几乎有近百家是售卖腌菜的商户。

paragraphs.en: “For us, suddenly, it became alive,” Mulder said. “A guy is waking up early in the morning and saying to his wife, ‘I go to my work.’ ”
paragraphs.zh: “对我们来说，突然间它有了生命，”穆尔德说。“一个男人清晨醒来，跟妻子说：‘我去上班了。’ ”

paragraphs.en: The pair showed the photos to Niki Russ Federman, a fourth-generation owner of Russ & Daughters, who was wearing the shop’s traditional white lab coat and doling out pickled herring in cream sauce. Her Polish-émigré great-grandfather got his start selling schmaltz herring wrapped in day-old Yiddish newspapers from a barrel, then upgraded to a pushcart, then to a horse-drawn cart, then to the original store, around the corner.
paragraphs.zh: 这对夫妇把照片给了 Russ & Daughters 的第四代店主 Niki Russ Federman 看，她穿着店里传统的白色实验服，在店里盛着奶油酱给人舀着腌鲱鱼。她的波兰移民高祖父最初是用一桶装的鲱鱼，用前一天的意第绪语报纸包好来卖，后来换成了流动车，再到马车，最后在街角开了第一家店。

paragraphs.en: “I’ve never seen it zoomed in like this!” Russ Federman said, peering at an image. “This was the J. Russ National Appetizing Store—there was nothing national about what he did. It was taking care of Jews within four square blocks.” Joel savvily rebranded as Russ’s Cut Rate Appetizing. Now Russ & Daughters has a nationwide shipping operation and a restaurant that serves eighteen-dollar Break-Fast Martinis.
paragraphs.zh: “我从来没见过这样放大的照片！”Russo Federman 说着凑近一张图片。“那是 J. Russ 全国开胃小铺——他的生意一点也不‘全国化’。他只是在四个街区范围内照顾犹太社区。” 乔尔很聪明地把它改头换面，成了 Russ’s Cut Rate Appetizing。现在，Russ & Daughters 已经发展成拥有全国配送业务的品牌，还开设了一家餐厅，供应 18 美元一杯的 Break-Fast Martinis。

paragraphs.en: “Why are you still here?” van Ravestein asked. “Is it resilience or flexibility?”
paragraphs.zh: “你还在这儿干嘛？”van Ravestein 问道。“是韧性？还是灵活性？”

paragraphs.en: “Luckily, whether it was by choice or by obligation, in each generation there’s been someone in the family who made this their life,” Russ Federman replied. In 1935, the store became the first in the country to have “& Daughters” in its name. “Joel needed cheap labor,” Russ Federman said. “But also, who better to sell pickles than three cute girls with their hands in herring barrels?”
paragraphs.zh: “幸运的是，无论是出于选择还是被迫，每一代家里都有人把这项事业当作终身事业，”罗斯·费德曼回答道。“1935 年，这家店成为全国第一家店名里带有“& Daughters”的商店。罗斯·费德曼说：‘乔尔需要廉价劳动力。’“而且，有谁比三个可爱的女孩更适合在腌鲱鱼桶里忙活、给人卖泡菜呢？”

paragraphs.en: “It’s a matter of communication, branding,” van Ravestein posited.
paragraphs.zh: “这关系到表达、品牌建设，”范·拉维斯坦说。

paragraphs.en: “You tell the story,” Mulder added. “The food is an interface that is also your legacy.”
paragraphs.zh: “你得讲好这个故事，”穆尔德补充道。“食物不仅是一种界面，也是你的传承。”

paragraphs.en: Van Ravestein munched on a half-sour and recalled an article, from 1890, that likened pickles to alcohol or coffee: “So kids couldn’t eat them, because it gives you a kick.”
paragraphs.zh: 范·拉维斯坦一边嚼着半酸口味的泡菜，一边回忆起一篇 1890 年的文章，该文把泡菜比作酒精或咖啡，说“所以孩子们不能吃，因为它会让你上头。”

paragraphs.en: Russ Federman mentioned a contemporary listicle of ten “superfoods,” most of which were sold at her store: “It’s herring, sardines, salmon, sauerkraut, liver—caviar was on the list!”
paragraphs.zh: Russ Federman 提到一篇当下流行的“十种超级食物”清单（listicle），其中大多是她店里有售的东西：“有鲱鱼、沙丁鱼、三文鱼、酸菜、肝脏——鱼子酱也在上面！”

paragraphs.en: “You know what we had yesterday evening?” Mulder asked, as she sipped a shrub (pickled-beet juice and vinegar, popular with the marathon-runner set). “A vermouth with pickle juice.”
paragraphs.zh: “你知道我们昨晚吃了什么吗？” Mulder边喝着 shrub 说道（用腌甜菜汁和醋调成的饮料，在马拉松跑步爱好者圈子里很流行），“一杯加了腌菜汁的苦艾酒。”

paragraphs.en: The Dutch visitors headed south to Sweet Pickle Books, on Orchard Street, a hip used bookstore with a pickle side hustle that was opened, in 2020, by a millennial named Leigh Altshuler, who previously served as marketing director for the Strand. Shoved in a corner was an adult-size pickle costume by the designer who made Katy Perry’s Super Bowl halftime-show sharks.
paragraphs.zh: 那对荷兰游客后来南下去了 Orchard Street 的 Sweet Pickle Books，这是一家时髦的二手书店，兼营腌菜生意，由一位名叫 Leigh Altshuler 的千禧一代于 2020 年开设，她此前在 Strand 担任过市场总监。店里角落里被塞着一套成年人尺寸的腌黄瓜造型服装，出自为 Katy Perry 的超级碗中场秀设计鲨鱼的人。

paragraphs.en: Before opening her store, Altshuler had prepared three hundred and sixty jars of pickles to sell, and eventually started giving them away to people who donated books. “I was, like, ‘This for sure will last me forever. It’ll be a gimmick,’ ” she told the Dutch duo. A year later, her shop was bursting at the seams, and she kept selling out of pickles. “People had been putting it on TikTok,” she said. Altshuler had to outsource pickle production upstate, and began cranking out pickle merch. “All of a sudden, Harry Styles was wearing it,” she said. “It was a whole drama.”
paragraphs.zh: 开店前，Altshuler 曾准备了三百六十罐腌菜打算卖掉，后来又开始把它们送给捐书的人。她对这对荷兰游客说：“我当时想，‘这肯定能让我持续很久。肯定是个噱头。’” 一年后，她的店人满为患，腌菜也总是售罄。“那时候大家开始在 TikTok 上发视频了，”她说。Altshuler 于是把腌菜生产外包到州外，并开始大量生产相关周边。她说：“突然间，Harry Styles 也穿起来了。”“这一整件事闹得很热闹。”

paragraphs.en: Mulder and van Ravestein mentioned the frenzy surrounding the Heinz pickle pin, a coveted souvenir at the 1939 New York World’s Fair.
paragraphs.zh: 穆尔德和范·拉维斯坦提到了围绕亨茨“泡菜徽章”所引发的狂热——这是1939年纽约世界博览会上备受青睐的一件纪念品。

paragraphs.en: “People are obsessed with pickles!” Altshuler said. “People come in and say, ‘Oh, look at my pickle tattoo,’ or, ‘My dog’s name is Pickle.’ ”
paragraphs.zh: “人们对泡菜着了魔！”奥尔特修勒说。“人们走进来就会说：‘哦，看看我的泡菜纹身。’或者，‘我家狗叫泡菜。’”

paragraphs.en: “In Holland, a lot of young people, they’ve started fermenting at home,” Mulder added.
paragraphs.zh: 穆尔德补充说：“在荷兰，很多年轻人已经开始在家自己发酵了。”

paragraphs.en: The Sweet Pickle Books building—near where “Lower East Side cool girlies” (per Altshuler) had dumpster-dived for Timothée Chalamet’s old “Marty Supreme” costumes—has been in its landlady’s family for a century and a half. Altshuler said, “Her grandfather sold melons from a pushcart outside.” ♦
paragraphs.zh: “低东区酷女孩”曾在这里翻找过蒂莫西·夏拉梅前“马蒂·最高”服装（按奥尔特修勒的说法）的甜菜泡菜书屋大楼，已由其女房东的家族经营了一个半世纪。奥尔特修勒说：“她的祖父曾在门外的手推车上卖甜瓜。” ♦
